questions.question: What character represents the current selector in CSS Nesting?
questions.explanation: The & (ampersand) represents the current parent selector in CSS Nesting.

questions.question: When is the & selector required in CSS Nesting?
questions.explanation: & is needed when combining with pseudo-classes (:hover), adjacent selectors (+), or modifiers (.active).

questions.question: Can you nest @media queries inside CSS rules?
questions.explanation: CSS Nesting allows @media queries to be nested inside rules.

questions.question: What is a best practice for nesting depth?
questions.explanation: Keep nesting to 2-3 levels to avoid excessive specificity.

questions.question: Does CSS nesting require a preprocessor?
questions.explanation: CSS Nesting is now native in modern browsers (Chrome 120+, Firefox 117+).

questions.question: What happens to specificity with deep nesting?
questions.explanation: Each nesting level increases specificity, which can make overrides difficult.

questions.question: Can you nest ::before and ::after pseudo-elements?
questions.explanation: Pseudo-elements can be nested: .card { &::before { ... } }

questions.question: Which of these is valid CSS Nesting syntax?
questions.explanation: .card { .title { } } is the nesting syntax; the others are flat.

questions.question: What should you avoid when using CSS Nesting?
questions.explanation: Deep nesting (3+ levels) should be avoided to maintain low specificity.

questions.question: Is CSS Nesting similar to Sass/SCSS nesting?
questions.explanation: CSS Nesting is similar but has some differences from Sass (e.g., & is required in more cases).

questions.question: What does the nested selector .card { & .title { } } resolve to?
questions.explanation: & .title inside .card resolves to .card .title (descendant selector).

questions.question: Can you nest @supports inside CSS rules?
questions.explanation: @supports can be nested inside rules just like @media in CSS Nesting.

questions.question: What does @scope combined with nesting provide?
questions.explanation: @scope with nesting provides component-level isolation, preventing style leakage.

questions.question: Which CSS preprocessor feature is NOT available in native CSS nesting?
questions.explanation: Native CSS nesting does not support @extend, @at-root, or property nesting.

questions.question: What does .button { &.active { } } resolve to?
questions.explanation: &.active concatenates: .button.active (element with both classes).

questions.question: In which Chrome version was CSS nesting supported?
questions.explanation: CSS nesting is supported in Chrome 120+, Firefox 117+, Safari 17.2+, and Edge 120+.

questions.question: What happens if you omit & before ::before in nesting?
options: It may still work in some browsers but using & is safer
It selects a child element instead
questions.explanation: Some browsers may allow omitting & for pseudo-elements, but including it is safer and more readable.

questions.question: Can @keyframes be nested inside CSS rules?
options: No, @keyframes must be at the stylesheet top level
questions.explanation: @keyframes cannot be nested inside rule sets; they must be at the stylesheet root level.

questions.question: What is a benefit of nesting @media queries inside selectors?
options: Co-located responsive logic makes CSS easier to maintain
questions.explanation: Nesting @media inside rules keeps related responsive styles together, improving maintainability.

questions.question: What tool can transpile CSS nesting for older browser support?
questions.explanation: The PostCSS Nesting plugin transpiles nested CSS to flat CSS for older browsers.
